attributes.stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-year history of intermittent difficulty in swallowing, which has recently become more problematic and is now associated with regurgitation. He has difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids. He also has some retrosternal discomfort that is unresponsive to a trial of omeprazole. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He gives no history of travel, is a non-smoker and drinks the occasional beer. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His BMI is 23 kg/m2. A contrast study is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The contrast study shows a massively dilated oesophagus, with pooling of contrast. There is smooth, consistent tapered narrowing of the distal oesophagus. These findings, together with the clinical picture are synonymous with achalasia. Achalasia is an oesophageal motility disorder due to increased tone at the lower oesophageal sphincter and disordered or lack of oesophageal peristalsis. The cause is unknown.

An oesophageal stricture is a narrowing of the oesophagus that can be caused by various factors such as gastroesophageal reflux disease, radiation therapy, or ingestion of caustic substances. While it can cause dysphagia, it typically presents as a focal narrowing on contrast studies, rather than the diffuse narrowing and dilation seen here. Additionally, strictures often cause more difficulty with solids than liquids, unlike the equal difficulty with both seen in this case.

Pseudoachalasia is uncommon and usually due to submucosal infiltration by a gastric or oesophago-gastric junctional cancer. With the four-year history in this scenario, the diagnosis of pseudoachalasia is unlikely.

Chagas disease is the result of a protozoan parasitic infection - Trypanosoma cruzi. The disease is endemic to South America, where it kills more people every year than any other parasitic infection - including malaria. The acute phase of Chagas disease is associated with nonspecific symptoms such as malaise, fever, and anorexia. However, chronic infection can lead to oesophageal and colonic dysmotility and cardiac problems. This disease is very rare in Australia, but with increasing international travel, taking a travel history and/or being aware of country of origin is imperative. The presentation and contrast study are consistent with Chagas disease but the history is not.

Diffuse oesophageal spasm can cause dysphagia and chest pain, but it typically presents with intermittent symptoms rather than the progressive course seen here. On contrast studies, it classically shows a 'corkscrew' or 'rosary bead' appearance of the oesophagus, not the smooth tapering and dilation seen in this case.

In this case, the contrast study demonstrates a gradual, tapering, smooth, conical narrowing of the distal oesophagus (so-called 'bird's beak' or 'rat tail' appearance) extending over 1-3 cm in length which is a classical achalasia pattern. This, combined with the clinical history of progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids, regurgitation, and chest discomfort unresponsive to proton pump inhibitors, strongly supports the diagnosis of achalasia.
learningPoints: Achalasia is an oesophageal motility disorder characterised by impaired relaxation of the lower oesophageal sphincter in response to swallowing and altered or absence of peristalsis, leading to a functional obstruction at the gastro-oesophageal junction.

Achalasia should be suspected in patients with dysphagia, heartburn unresponsive to a trial of proton pump inhibitor therapy, and unusually increased resistance to the passage of an endoscope through the oesophago-gastric junction in the absence of a mucosal lesion.
references: Engel LS, Chow WH, Vaughan TL, et al. Population attributable risks of esophageal and gastric cancers. J Natl Cancer Inst 2003; 95:1404. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/13130116/

Sadowski DC, Ackah F, Jiang B, Svenson LW. Achalasia: incidence, prevalence and survival. A population-based study. Neurogastroenterol Motil 2010; 22:e256. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20465592/

de Oliveira RB, Rezende Filho J, Dantas RO, Iazigi N. The spectrum of esophageal motor disorders in Chagas' disease. Am J Gastroenterol 1995; 90:1119. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7611209/

Csendes A, Braghetto I, Burdiles P, et al. Very late results of esophagomyotomy for patients with achalasia: clinical, endoscopic, histologic, manometric, and acid reflux studies in 67 patients for a mean follow-up of 190 months. Ann Surg 2006; 243:196. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16432352/

Streitz JM Jr, Ellis FH Jr, Gibb SP, Heatley GM. Achalasia and squamous cell carcinoma of the esophagus: analysis of 241 patients. Ann Thorac Surg 1995; 59:1604. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7771859/
Bern C, Martin DL, Gilman RH. Acute and congenital Chagas disease. Adv Parasitol 2011; 75:19. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21820550/
Kahrilas PJ, Kishk SM, Helm JF, et al. Comparison of pseudoachalasia and achalasia. Am J Med 1987; 82:439. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/3548347/
